facts.text: The MOS 6502 CPU powered systems like the NES, Commodore 64, and Atari 2600.
The Zilog Z80 processor was widely used in arcade machines and home computers.
The Motorola 68000 CPU powered the Sega Genesis and many arcade systems.
The PlayStation used a custom R3000A MIPS CPU at its core.
The Nintendo 64 used a 64-bit NEC VR4300 processor.
The SNES used a Ricoh 5A22 CPU based on the 65C816 architecture.
The Atari 2600 used the MOS 6507, a stripped-down version of the 6502.
The Sega Saturn used dual Hitachi SH-2 processors.
The Dreamcast used a Hitachi SH-4 CPU paired with a PowerVR GPU.
The Xbox used a custom Intel Pentium III processor.
The PlayStation 2 used the Emotion Engine, a custom MIPS-based CPU.
The GameCube used an IBM PowerPC Gekko processor.
The Wii reused a modified PowerPC-based Broadway CPU.
The Wii U used a tri-core IBM PowerPC Espresso CPU.
The Switch uses a custom NVIDIA Tegra X1 system-on-chip.
The NES PPU (Picture Processing Unit) handled sprite rendering and scrolling.
The SNES Mode 7 chip enabled background scaling and rotation effects.
The Atari 2600 had no frame buffer and rendered graphics line-by-line.
The PS1 GPU supported textured polygons and affine transformations.
The N64 used a Reality Coprocessor for 3D graphics acceleration.
The Neo Geo hardware was essentially an arcade system in home console form.
The Neo Geo AES and MVS shared identical hardware architecture.
The Sega Model 2 arcade board powered games like Daytona USA.
The Sega Model 3 board was one of the most powerful arcade systems of its time.
The Naomi arcade system was based on Dreamcast architecture.
The CPS-1 arcade board powered early Capcom classics like Street Fighter II.
The CPS-2 board introduced stronger encryption to prevent arcade game piracy.
The CPS-3 system powered games like Street Fighter III.
The Zilog Z80 was often used as a sound CPU in arcade systems.
Arcade machines often used multiple CPUs for graphics, sound, and game logic.
The Game Boy used a custom Sharp LR35902 CPU.
The Game Boy Color used an upgraded version of the Sharp CPU with color support.
The Game Boy Advance used a 32-bit ARM7TDMI processor.
The PSP used a MIPS R4000-based CPU architecture.
The PS Vita used a quad-core ARM Cortex-A9 CPU.
The Atari Jaguar used a multi-chip architecture with Tom and Jerry processors.
The Atari Lynx was co-developed by Epyx and Atari engineers.
The WonderSwan used a low-power NEC V30MZ CPU.
The WonderSwan Color improved display hardware over the original model.
The Neo Geo Pocket used a Toshiba TLCS-900H CPU.
The Neo Geo Pocket Color improved on the original with a full-color LCD.
The Game Gear used a Zilog Z80 CPU similar to the Master System.
The Sega Master System also used a Zilog Z80 CPU.
The NES sound chip was the Ricoh 2A03, which included audio synthesis.
The SNES used a dedicated audio DSP for advanced sound effects.
The PS3 Cell processor used a unique multi-core architecture with SPE units.
The Xbox 360 used a triple-core Xenon CPU based on IBM PowerPC.
The PS4 used a semi-custom AMD Jaguar CPU and Radeon GPU.
The PS5 uses a custom AMD Zen 2 CPU and RDNA 2 GPU.
Modern consoles use unified memory architectures for CPU and GPU efficiency.
The PlayStation 2 GPU, known as the Graphics Synthesizer, had extremely high fill-rate for its time.
The PS2 Emotion Engine included vector units for physics and geometry calculations.
The Nintendo 64 Reality Coprocessor handled both graphics and audio tasks.
The Dreamcast PowerVR GPU used tile-based rendering to optimize performance.
The Sega Saturn used two video display processors for 2D and 3D rendering.
The Atari Jaguar included a GPU called Tom and a DSP called Jerry.
The Neo Geo had dedicated sprite hardware capable of handling large on-screen objects.
Arcade boards often used separate chips for sound, video, and game logic.
The CPS-2 board used encryption chips to prevent game copying and bootlegging.
The CPS-3 system required a security cartridge for game decryption at boot.
The Sega Model 3 arcade board was capable of near-real-time 3D rendering in the 1990s.
The Sega Naomi system used interchangeable ROM boards similar to Dreamcast GD-ROMs.
The Taito Type X arcade system was based on PC hardware architecture.
The CPS-1 arcade board helped define Capcom’s golden era of arcade games.
Many arcade boards used Motorola 68000 CPUs for main processing.
The NES PPU could only display 64 sprites at once.
The SNES Mode 7 allowed flat backgrounds to simulate 3D rotation.
The Game Boy screen resolution was only 160x144 pixels.
The Game Boy Advance had a resolution of 240x160 pixels.
The PSP screen resolution was 480x272 pixels.
The PS Vita featured a 960x544 OLED display in early models.
The Nintendo DS used two separate 256x192 pixel screens.
The 3DS top screen could display stereoscopic 3D without glasses.
The Wii GPU was based on an ATI Hollywood chipset.
The GameCube GPU was called 'Flipper' and was co-developed with ATI.
The PS3 GPU was based on NVIDIA’s RSX architecture.
The Xbox 360 GPU introduced unified shader architecture.
The PS4 and Xbox One both adopted AMD-based GPU architectures.
The PS5 GPU supports ray tracing acceleration in hardware.
Modern consoles use GDDR memory shared between CPU and GPU.
The Atari 2600 used a Television Interface Adapter for video output.
The Atari 2600 required developers to manage scanlines manually.
The Sega Genesis supported hardware scrolling for smoother motion.
The Neo Geo could display 380 sprites simultaneously on screen.
The Dreamcast VMU used a low-power LCD for mini-display functions.
The Game Boy used a custom LCD with no backlight.
The Game Boy Color added a reflective color LCD panel.
The Game Gear screen was backlit, but power-hungry.
The Lynx had a wide-angle LCD display for better viewing angles.
The WonderSwan used a highly efficient low-power LCD design.
The NES audio system used 5 channels for sound synthesis.
The SNES supported sample-based audio via its SPC700 chip.
The Genesis had FM synthesis audio via the Yamaha YM2612 chip.
The PS1 included a dedicated SPU for sound processing.
The N64 had advanced audio processing for real-time 3D sound effects.
Arcade machines often used CRT monitors tuned for specific refresh rates.
Many arcade boards were designed for coin-operated durability and heat resistance.
The CPS-2 system used battery-backed encryption keys that could expire.
The Sega Model 2 board was based on Sega’s partnership with Lockheed Martin.
Arcade hardware evolution drove early innovation in 3D gaming technology.
The Sega Saturn used a separate sound processor called the Yamaha FH1.
The PlayStation GPU used a fixed 1 MB of VRAM for textures and frame buffers.
The Nintendo 64 had only 4 KB of texture cache on its RCP.
The Dreamcast supported GD-ROM discs with higher capacity than CDs.
The Xbox original used a modified Windows NT kernel internally.
The PS2 used 32 MB of system RAM, which was extremely limited even for its time.
The GameCube used proprietary mini-discs instead of standard DVDs.
The Wii Remote used infrared LEDs tracked by a sensor bar for pointing control.
The Wii U GamePad included a built-in touchscreen controller display.
The Nintendo Switch supports both handheld and docked GPU modes.
The Atari 2600 joystick port became a de facto standard for later controllers.
The Atari 800 home computer shared architecture concepts with arcade systems.
The Commodore 64 used the SID chip for advanced sound synthesis.
The SID chip is still considered one of the most iconic sound chips ever made.
The Amiga used the Paula chip for audio and DMA-based data transfer.
The Amiga’s graphics chipset supported multiple display modes simultaneously.
The ZX Spectrum used a simple framebuffer with attribute-based color limitations.
The MSX standard unified hardware across multiple manufacturers in the 1980s.
The PC Engine used HuCards instead of traditional cartridges.
The Neo Geo AES cartridges could cost several hundred dollars at launch.
The CPS-2 system stored encryption keys in suicide battery-backed memory.
The CPS-3 system required CD-ROM based game loading with security checks.
The Sega Naomi 2 added improved geometry processing over Naomi 1.
The Taito Type X systems used standard PC GPUs for arcade flexibility.
The Atomiswave system was based on Sega Dreamcast architecture.
The Nintendo DS used ARM9 and ARM7 dual processors for split workloads.
The 3DS added a dedicated GPU for stereoscopic rendering.
The PSP used a Media Engine co-processor for multimedia decoding.
The PS Vita included a dedicated GPU alongside its ARM CPU.
The Game Boy Advance SP added a backlight in later revisions.
The Game Boy Color CPU ran at roughly double the speed of the original Game Boy.
The Game Gear’s color LCD made it more advanced but less power efficient than Game Boy.
The Lynx supported hardware scaling and sprite zooming in real time.
The WonderSwan’s design emphasized ultra-low power consumption.
The Neo Geo Pocket Color used a 16-bit CPU optimized for fighting games.
The Sega Dreamcast controller included a built-in VMU slot.
The VMU could function independently as a mini handheld device.
The NES Zapper used light sensing to detect CRT screen flashes.
The SNES Super FX chip enabled early 3D rendering in games like Star Fox.
The Sega Genesis had an optional Sega CD add-on for expanded storage.
The Sega 32X add-on attempted to bridge Genesis and 32-bit generation.
The Philips CD-i was an early multimedia console with CD-based software.
The Virtual Boy used red LED displays for stereoscopic 3D effects.
The Vectrex used a vector display instead of raster graphics.
The Intellivision was a competitor to the Atari 2600 with superior graphics.
The ColecoVision brought arcade-quality graphics to home consoles in the 1980s.
The Magnavox Odyssey was the first commercial home video game console.
The Fairchild Channel F was the first console to use programmable cartridges.
The Bally Astrocade featured advanced color graphics for its era.
Early consoles often relied on custom hardware rather than standardized CPUs.
The PlayStation 2 had built-in support for DVD playback without needing additional hardware.
The PS2’s Vector Units were heavily used for animation and physics calculations.
The Nintendo GameCube used a custom optical disc format to discourage piracy.
The Wii’s Broadway CPU was derived from the GameCube’s architecture.
The Wii U struggled due to limited third-party hardware optimization.
The Nintendo Switch Joy-Cons include HD rumble for advanced haptic feedback.
The Switch uses a hybrid architecture enabling docked and handheld performance scaling.
The Xbox original supported built-in hard drives, uncommon at the time.
The Xbox 360 introduced detachable hard drives for storage expansion.
The PS3’s Cell processor was extremely powerful but difficult to develop for.
The PSP UMD format was unique to Sony handhelds and never widely adopted elsewhere.
The PS Vita supported both physical cartridges and digital downloads.
The Nintendo DS Lite improved screen brightness dramatically over the original DS.
The 3DS’s autostereoscopic 3D effect required precise viewing angles.
The Game Boy Advance SP was the first Nintendo handheld with a built-in rechargeable battery.
The Game Boy line used link cables for multiplayer connectivity.
The Game Boy Color allowed limited palette changes for original Game Boy games.
The Game Gear TV Tuner allowed users to watch analog television on the device.
The Atari Lynx supported linking up to 17 devices in multiplayer sessions.
The WonderSwan had an incredibly long battery life due to efficient hardware design.
The Neo Geo Pocket Color had a micro-switched joystick for precise input.
The Neo Geo AES used identical hardware to arcade MVS systems.
The Sega Genesis had expansion ports for accessories like the Sega CD.
The Sega CD added full-motion video capabilities to Genesis games.
The Sega 32X was designed to extend Genesis life into 32-bit gaming.
The Atari 2600 joystick became one of the most recognizable controller designs ever.
The Atari 5200 controller used analog input but was notoriously unreliable.
The Intellivision controller used a numeric keypad instead of a joystick.
The ColecoVision expansion module allowed Atari 2600 compatibility.
The Vectrex included a built-in vector monitor, eliminating the need for a TV.
The Philips CD-i supported multimedia applications beyond gaming.
The Virtual Boy required a stand and viewed games through dual mirrors.
The Sega Dreamcast supported broadband modem accessories in some regions.
The PS1 memory card stored only 1 MB of save data.
The Nintendo 64 controller introduced a central analog stick design.
The SNES Super Game Boy allowed Game Boy games to be played on TV.
The NES Advantage controller was designed for arcade-style gameplay.
The Sega Master System had a light gun accessory called the Light Phaser.
The Atari Jaguar CD add-on extended storage capacity for larger games.
The Neo Geo memory card allowed arcade progress to be saved at home.
The Game Boy Camera included built-in mini-games and editing tools.
The PSP Go removed the UMD drive in favor of digital downloads.
The PS Vita Slim improved ergonomics and reduced weight.
The Nintendo DS could download demos and content via Nintendo Wi-Fi Connection.
The 3DS eShop allowed digital distribution of indie and retro games.
The Xbox Kinect used infrared depth sensing for motion tracking.
The PlayStation Move used motion tracking with colored orb controllers.
The Wii Balance Board measured weight distribution for fitness games.
Arcade cabinets often included DIP switches to configure difficulty and settings.
Hardware evolution in gaming consistently pushed graphics and memory limits forward.
Pac-Man was originally called 'Puck-Man' in Japan before being renamed for Western release.
Donkey Kong was one of the first games to feature a jumping mechanic as core gameplay.
The original Street Fighter had pressure-sensitive punch and kick pads in the arcade cabinet.
Galaga was a sequel to Galaxian and introduced the ability to capture your own ship.
Space Invaders was so popular it caused a shortage of 100-yen coins in Japan.
The NES was known as the Famicom in Japan, short for Family Computer.
The Nintendo Entertainment System helped revive the video game industry after the 1983 crash.
Super Mario Bros. helped define side-scrolling platform games.
The NES controller helped popularize the D-pad design.
The Legend of Zelda introduced battery-backed saving in cartridges.
The Sega Genesis was known as the Mega Drive outside North America.
Sonic the Hedgehog was designed to compete directly with Mario’s popularity.
The Genesis used a Motorola 68000 CPU similar to early home computers.
The Sega Genesis marketing slogan was 'Genesis does what Nintendon’t'.
Mortal Kombat on Genesis was famous for having uncensored blood via a cheat code.
The Atari 2600 helped establish interchangeable game cartridges as the standard.
Pitfall! was one of the earliest platform adventure games on home consoles.
The Atari 2600 joystick had a single button and a digital directional stick.
E.T. for Atari 2600 is often cited as part of the 1983 video game crash.
The Atari 2600 had only 128 bytes of RAM.
The PlayStation was originally a failed Nintendo collaboration project.
Final Fantasy VII helped establish cinematic storytelling in RPGs.
Metal Gear Solid pioneered stealth gameplay on consoles.
The PlayStation used CD-ROMs instead of cartridges to reduce production costs.
The original PlayStation sold over 100 million units worldwide.
The Nintendo 64 introduced analog control with its unique controller design.
Super Mario 64 was one of the first fully 3D platform games.
The Nintendo 64 used cartridges instead of CDs, limiting storage but improving load times.
GoldenEye 007 helped define console multiplayer FPS gaming.
The Nintendo 64 was named after its 64-bit processor architecture.
The Game Boy helped define portable gaming as a mainstream market.
Pokémon Red and Blue became cultural phenomena on the Game Boy.
The Game Boy had a famously long battery life compared to competitors.
The Game Boy’s monochrome screen was chosen to maximize battery life.
Tetris became one of the most iconic pack-in games in history.
The SNES introduced Mode 7 graphics for pseudo-3D effects.
Super Metroid is considered one of the best action-adventure games ever made.
The SNES controller introduced the X and Y face buttons.
Chrono Trigger featured multiple endings based on player choices.
The SNES is widely considered one of Nintendo’s greatest consoles.
The Dreamcast was the first console with built-in online play.
Sonic Adventure was a flagship launch title for the Dreamcast.
The Dreamcast VMU had a built-in screen for mini-games and stats.
The Dreamcast was Sega’s final home console.
Crazy Taxi became one of the Dreamcast’s most iconic arcade-style games.
The PS2 could play DVDs, making it a popular home entertainment device.
Grand Theft Auto: San Andreas became one of the best-selling PS2 games.
The PS2 had a massive game library with over 3,800 titles.
Shadow of the Colossus was praised for its artistic minimalist design.
The PS2 remains the best-selling console of all time.
Pac-Man was designed to appeal to a wider audience, including women, which was rare for arcade games at the time.
Donkey Kong was one of Nintendo’s first major arcade hits and introduced Mario as 'Jumpman'.
Space Invaders introduced the concept of increasing difficulty as enemies speed up.
Asteroids used vector graphics instead of sprite-based rendering.
Ms. Pac-Man was originally created as an unofficial enhancement kit for Pac-Man.
Street Fighter II popularized the fighting game genre worldwide.
Mortal Kombat used digitized actors for its character sprites.
Final Fight helped define the side-scrolling beat-em-up genre.
NBA Jam became famous for its exaggerated arcade-style basketball gameplay.
Pac-Land was one of the first arcade games to feature side-scrolling movement.
The Sega Saturn had strong 2D capabilities that made it popular for arcade ports.
Nights into Dreams showcased the Saturn’s graphical capabilities.
The Saturn’s dual-CPU design made it difficult for developers to optimize games.
The Saturn was released earlier than the PlayStation in North America, hurting its market performance.
Virtua Fighter helped establish 3D fighting games on consoles.
The Atari Jaguar was marketed as a 64-bit system despite debate over its architecture.
Alien vs Predator is one of the most notable Atari Jaguar games.
The Jaguar controller included a numeric keypad for extended input.
The Atari Jaguar struggled due to a small game library.
Tempest 2000 was a standout title showcasing the Jaguar’s capabilities.
The Neo Geo AES was considered a luxury console due to extremely expensive cartridges.
The King of Fighters series became one of SNK’s most iconic franchises.
Metal Slug is famous for its detailed pixel art and animation.
Neo Geo hardware was essentially arcade-perfect at home.
Samurai Shodown helped define weapon-based fighting games.
The Game Boy Camera allowed users to take and edit black-and-white photos.
The Game Boy Printer let users print images from the Game Boy Camera.
The Game Boy Pocket was a smaller, lighter revision of the original Game Boy.
The original Game Boy screen had no backlight, relying on ambient light.
The Game Boy helped establish portable gaming as a global phenomenon.
The PSP was one of the first handhelds to support full motion video playback.
God of War: Chains of Olympus was a major PSP exclusive.
The PSP had Wi-Fi connectivity for online multiplayer gaming.
The PSP Go was the first major Sony handheld to go fully digital.
Monster Hunter became extremely popular on PSP in Japan.
The PlayStation Vita featured dual analog sticks, a first for Sony handhelds.
Uncharted: Golden Abyss was a flagship PS Vita launch title.
The PS Vita had a rear touchpad for unique control schemes.
The PS Vita could connect to the PS3 and PS4 for remote play.
Persona 4 Golden is widely considered one of the best PS Vita games.
The Nintendo DS introduced PictoChat for local messaging between devices.
Mario Kart DS helped popularize online handheld racing games.
The DS had two separate screens that enabled unique gameplay mechanics.
Brain Age became a surprise hit that expanded the DS audience.
The Nintendo DS helped bring casual gaming into the mainstream.
The Nintendo 3DS initially launched with a high price before a rapid price cut.
Animal Crossing: New Leaf became a defining 3DS title.
The 3DS introduced augmented reality cards for interactive gameplay.
The New Nintendo 3DS improved performance for more demanding games.
The Nintendo 3DS succeeded despite competition from smartphones.
The original Xbox used a custom 733 MHz Intel Pentium III processor and 64 MB of RAM, making it the most powerful console of its generation.
The original Xbox included a built-in 8 GB hard drive, the first console to ship with one as standard, enabling game saves without memory cards.
The Xbox 360 shipped in two configurations at launch: a Core system without a hard drive and a Premium bundle with a 20 GB detachable drive.
The Xbox 360's three-core IBM Xenon CPU ran at 3.2 GHz and used a unified shader architecture that was ahead of its time for consoles.
The Xbox 360 Red Ring of Death failure rate was estimated at 54% by some analysts; Microsoft extended warranties to three years and set aside over $1 billion to cover repairs.
The Xbox One X in 2017 featured 6 teraflops of GPU performance and 12 GB of GDDR5 RAM, making it the most powerful console hardware available at its release.
The Xbox Series X uses a custom AMD Zen 2 CPU with 8 cores at 3.8 GHz and an RDNA 2 GPU delivering 12 teraflops, paired with 16 GB of GDDR6 memory.
The Xbox 360's GPU was an ATI Xenos chip designed specifically for the console, featuring 48 unified shaders — a design that influenced the PC graphics industry.
The PS3's Cell Broadband Engine consisted of one Power Processing Element and seven Synergistic Processing Elements, delivering a theoretical 218 GFLOPS.
The PS3 launched at $499 for the 20 GB model and $599 for the 60 GB model in 2006, making it one of the most expensive console launches in history.
The original 60 GB PS3 included hardware PS2 backward compatibility using a secondary Emotion Engine chip; later slim models removed this entirely.
The PS4 Pro added a second GPU cluster to reach 4.2 teraflops of compute performance, up from the base PS4's 1.84 teraflops.
The PS5 uses a custom AMD Zen 2 CPU at 3.5 GHz with an RDNA 2 GPU at 10.28 teraflops, paired with an ultra-fast 825 GB NVMe SSD achieving 5.5 GB/s.
The PS5's DualSense controller introduced haptic feedback motors replacing traditional rumble, plus adaptive triggers with variable resistance controlled by the game.
Sony's PS Vita used an ARM Cortex-A9 quad-core CPU and a quad-core PowerVR SGX543MP4+ GPU, making it the most powerful handheld at its 2012 launch.
The PS Vita used proprietary memory cards that were significantly more expensive than standard SD cards, widely cited as a key factor in its poor sales.
The Nintendo 64 used a 64-bit NEC VR4300 CPU running at 93.75 MHz, but its 9 MB of total RAM was a bottleneck that developers frequently cited.
The N64's Reality Co-Processor (RCP) combined both the graphics processor and the audio processor into a single custom chip designed by SGI.
The GameCube's custom IBM PowerPC Gekko CPU ran at 485 MHz and was paired with an ATI Flipper GPU; both chips were designed specifically for Nintendo.
The GameCube's proprietary 8 cm optical discs held 1.5 GB of data and were chosen partly to prevent easy piracy, as standard DVD drives couldn't read them.
The Wii's main processor was a 729 MHz IBM Broadway chip — essentially a refined version of the GameCube's Gekko — maintaining backward compatibility.
The Wii Remote used a Broadcom Bluetooth chip and a PixArt optical sensor to detect the IR LEDs in the sensor bar, not any motion from the bar itself.
The Wii U's GamePad featured a 6.2-inch touchscreen and used a custom 802.11n 5 GHz band to stream video from the console at up to 60 fps with very low latency.
The Nintendo Switch's NVIDIA Tegra X1 SoC runs at reduced clock speeds when docked (1 GHz CPU / 768 MHz GPU) versus handheld mode (1 GHz CPU / 307 MHz GPU) to manage heat.
The Nintendo Switch cartridges use a coating that tastes extremely bitter to deter children from putting them in their mouths, a deliberate safety design decision.
The Nintendo 3DS uses two separate ARM processors: the new ARM11 MPCore for main processing and the original Nintendo DS's ARM9 and ARM7 for backward compatibility.
The New Nintendo 3DS upgraded the CPU to a quad-core ARM11 at 804 MHz from the original's single-core 268 MHz, and added a second analog nub called the C-stick.
The Game Boy Advance SP introduced a clamshell design, a front-lit (later backlit) screen, and a rechargeable lithium-ion battery — all significant improvements over the original GBA.
The Game Boy Micro, released in 2005, was the smallest Game Boy ever at 50 mm × 101 mm and weighed only 80 grams, but lacked backward compatibility with original Game Boy games.
The Nintendo DS used two separate ARM processors — ARM9 at 67 MHz and ARM7 at 33 MHz — plus two displays, a touchscreen, and a microphone, all in a clamshell body.
The Sega Genesis used a Motorola 68000 CPU at 7.6 MHz as the main processor and a Zilog Z80 at 3.58 MHz as a secondary audio processor.
The Sega Mega Drive (Genesis) had a palette of 512 colors but could only display 61 on screen simultaneously without special programming tricks.
The Sega Saturn used two Hitachi SH-2 CPUs running in parallel at 28.6 MHz each, a design that proved notoriously difficult for developers to program efficiently.
The Saturn's VDP1 chip rendered quadrilateral polygons rather than triangles, which was unusual and contributed to some visual distortion compared to competitors.
The Dreamcast used a Hitachi SH-4 CPU at 200 MHz and a custom NEC PowerVR2 CLX2 GPU capable of 3 million polygons per second.
The Dreamcast was the first console to ship standard with a built-in 56K modem for online play, though the US version used a 33.6K modem at launch.
The Sega Game Gear used the same processor as the Master System — a 3.58 MHz Zilog Z80 — making it essentially a portable Master System with a backlit color screen.
The Game Gear's backlit color LCD consumed battery power rapidly; it required 6 AA batteries and typically lasted only 3 to 5 hours per set.
The Sega CD (Mega-CD) added a 12.5 MHz Motorola 68000 as a coprocessor to the Genesis, along with 6 Mbit of additional RAM and a hardware scaler/rotator chip.
The Sega 32X contained two Hitachi SH-2 CPUs at 23 MHz and could display up to 32,768 colors, but its complex setup requiring both Sega CD and Genesis limited its appeal.
The Atari 2600 used a MOS 6507 CPU at 1.19 MHz — a cost-reduced variant of the 6502 with fewer address lines — paired with the custom TIA graphics chip.
The Atari 2600's TIA chip had no frame buffer; it generated the video signal in real time one scanline at a time, requiring programmers to race the electron beam.
The Atari 5200 used an Atari 400/800 computer architecture with a 1.79 MHz MOS 6502C CPU and the GTIA graphics chip, but lacked the 2600's joystick port compatibility.
The Atari Jaguar's packaging and marketing claimed 64-bit performance, but the CPU (Motorola 68000) was 32-bit; the 64-bit claim referred to its custom object processor bus width.
The Atari Jaguar included two custom processors named 'Tom' (graphics) and 'Jerry' (audio/DSP), plus a 26.6 MHz Motorola 68000 as the main CPU.
The Atari Lynx was the world's first color handheld game console, featuring a 16-bit custom Mikey CPU at 4 MHz and hardware sprite scaling and rotation.
The Atari Lynx's screen could be flipped 180 degrees so left-handed players could hold it comfortably — a unique ergonomic feature for its time.
The NES used a Ricoh 2A03 CPU running at 1.79 MHz, which was a modified MOS 6502 with the decimal mode disabled and an integrated audio processing unit.
The NES's PPU (Picture Processing Unit) supported a maximum of 64 sprites simultaneously but could only display 8 per scanline, causing the characteristic sprite flicker.
The NES cartridge 10NES lockout chip ran at 1 MHz and required a matching chip in the console to authenticate, causing many third-party publishers to clone or bypass it.
The SNES used a 16-bit Ricoh 5A22 CPU (based on the 65C816) running at up to 3.58 MHz, with a custom Sony SPC700 sound chip delivering 8 channels of sampled audio.
The SNES's Mode 7 graphics mode allowed the PPU to rotate and scale a single background layer, creating pseudo-3D perspective effects without additional hardware.
The SNES could display up to 256 colors on screen simultaneously from a palette of 32,768, a significant leap over the NES's 25 simultaneous colors from 54 total.
The Super FX chip, a custom RISC processor at 10.5 MHz embedded in certain SNES cartridges, enabled real-time 3D polygon rendering that the base console hardware couldn't perform.
The Sega Master System used a 3.58 MHz Zilog Z80 CPU and a Texas Instruments TMS9918-derived VDP that could display 64 colors from a 64-color palette.
The Sega Master System's Japanese version (Mark III) used a different cartridge connector than the Western versions, and the Japanese version required an FM sound unit add-on for enhanced audio.
The PC Engine (TurboGrafx-16) used a custom HuC6280 CPU based on the 65C02 at 7.16 MHz — faster than both the NES and early Genesis — with a powerful 16-color-per-sprite GPU.
The PC Engine's HuCard format was smaller than a credit card, making it the smallest game media of its era; the console itself was also notably compact.
The PC Engine CD-ROM² add-on, released in 1988, was the first CD-ROM peripheral for a home console and added 64 KB of RAM plus a CD audio streaming capability.
The Neo Geo AES home console used the exact same Motorola 68000 CPU at 12 MHz and Zilog Z80 audio processor as the MVS arcade hardware, ensuring true arcade-perfect conversions.
The Neo Geo AES supported up to 4 simultaneous players via a multi-tap accessory and featured two controller ports with 6-button arcade-style pads.
Neo Geo MVS arcade boards supported multiple game cartridges in a single cabinet (1 to 6 slots depending on the board), allowing operators to offer multiple games in one machine.
The Neo Geo CD, released in 1994, replaced cartridges with CD-ROM but featured a notoriously slow 1x CD drive causing load times of 30 to 60 seconds between rounds.
The Neo Geo Pocket Color used a custom 16-bit Toshiba TLCS900H CPU at 6 MHz and a secondary Z80 for sound, in a compact clamshell-adjacent design with a clicky micro-switch joystick.
The WonderSwan used a 16-bit NEC V30MZ CPU at 3.072 MHz — a variant of the Intel 8086 — consuming only a single AA battery for approximately 30 hours of play.
The WonderSwan Color upgraded the display to a TFT color LCD capable of 241 colors on screen from a 4,096-color palette, while retaining backward compatibility.
The WonderSwan could be oriented both horizontally and vertically thanks to its buttons on multiple edges, allowing games to use portrait mode for a taller play area.
The Commodore 64 used a 6510 CPU at 0.985 MHz (PAL) or 1.023 MHz (NTSC), with the MOS 6581 SID chip providing three voices of synthesis and a programmable filter.
The Commodore 64's VIC-II graphics chip could display 16 colors, 40×25 text, hardware sprites (up to 8 simultaneously), and smooth hardware scrolling.
The Commodore Amiga 500 used a Motorola 68000 at 7.16 MHz paired with three custom chips: Agnus (DMA and memory), Denise (video), and Paula (audio/IO).
The Amiga's custom chip set enabled hardware-assisted blitting — copying rectangular blocks of data in the background — freeing the CPU for other tasks simultaneously.
The Amiga 1200 upgraded to an AGA chip set with a 24-bit color palette (16.7 million colors) and could display up to 256,000 colors on screen using HAM-8 mode.
The ZX Spectrum used a Zilog Z80 CPU at 3.5 MHz and had only 16 KB or 48 KB of RAM; its rubber keyboard and attribute-based color system defined its character.
The ZX Spectrum's ULA (Uncommitted Logic Array) custom chip handled video generation, keyboard scanning, and cassette interface in a single chip to minimize component count.
The MSX standard was defined by Microsoft and ASCII Corporation in 1983 as a common hardware specification, ensuring software compatibility across machines from multiple manufacturers.
MSX computers used a Zilog Z80 CPU at 3.58 MHz and a Texas Instruments TMS9918 VDP; the MSX2 standard added the Yamaha V9938 chip with higher resolution and more colors.
CPS-1 (Capcom Play System 1) used a 10 MHz Motorola 68000 main CPU and a Zilog Z80 audio CPU, with a custom graphics chip capable of large multi-layered sprites.
CPS-2 introduced encrypted ROMs to prevent bootleg copies; each board contained a suicide battery that erased the decryption keys if tampered with or if the battery died.
CPS-3 used a custom SH-2-based CPU, SIMM memory modules instead of mask ROMs, and CD-ROM for software distribution — an unusual approach for dedicated arcade hardware.
The Sega Model 2 arcade board used a 25 MHz i960 CPU for geometry processing plus the Lockheed Martin Real3D hardware originally developed for military flight simulators.
Sega Model 3 used a PowerPC 603e at 66 MHz for the main CPU and a Sega-designed Real3D Pro-1000 GPU capable of over 2 million polygons per second.
The Sega NAOMI shared its CPU architecture with the Dreamcast — both used a Hitachi SH-4 at 200 MHz — but NAOMI had more RAM and a higher bandwidth memory bus.
NAOMI 2 added two PowerVR CLX2 GPUs working in parallel, doubling rendering throughput compared to the original NAOMI's single GPU.
The Sammy Atomiswave arcade platform was based on NAOMI hardware but used a proprietary cartridge format and targeted lower-budget developers needing cheaper arcade development.
Taito Type X used standard PC hardware (Intel Pentium 4 CPU, ATI Radeon GPU) in a ruggedized arcade enclosure, dramatically reducing development costs for arcade titles.
The original JAMMA wiring standard, established in 1985, defined a 56-pin edge connector that allowed arcade PCBs from different manufacturers to work in the same cabinet.
Early vector arcade monitors like those in Asteroids used deflection coils to draw lines directly with the electron beam rather than scanning a raster grid, producing crisp glowing lines.
Williams arcade hardware from the early 1980s used a Motorola 6809 CPU and custom hardware for raster graphics; their designs were influential for using a separate dedicated sound CPU.
Midway's Y-Unit arcade hardware (1990) used a TMS34010 graphics processor and 4 MB of ROM, enabling digitized photo-realistic sprite graphics in games of that era.
The System 16 arcade board from Sega used a 10 MHz Motorola 68000 and a Zilog Z80 for sound, with a tile-based background system and a dedicated sprite layer hardware.
SNK's MVS arcade hardware used large 330-pin cartridges holding up to 716 Mbit of ROM data, far exceeding the storage capacity of competing arcade systems of the same era.
Konami's GX arcade system board of the 1990s used a 68EC020 CPU and a custom tilemap chip, serving as the hardware platform for many of Konami's 2D arcade titles.
The original Game Boy's Sharp LR35902 CPU was a hybrid of the Intel 8080 and Zilog Z80 instruction sets, running at 4.194 MHz and consuming extremely little power.
The Game Boy's reflective LCD screen had no backlight to save battery life; Nintendo chose to make it sunlight-readable rather than battery-draining, extending runtime to approximately 15 hours.
The Game Boy Color added an infrared communication port above the cartridge slot for close-range data exchange between two units, used by a limited number of game cartridges.
The PSP used a MIPS R4000 CPU at 222 MHz (later unlocked to 333 MHz) paired with a custom Sony GPU; the wide 16:9 LCD screen was the largest on any handheld at launch.
The PSP's UMD (Universal Media Disc) optical format held up to 1.8 GB of data; the disc drive's slow spin-up time contributed to loading delays that flash-based competitors avoided.
The Game Boy Advance used a 32-bit ARM7TDMI CPU at 16.78 MHz plus a secondary Z80 at 8 MHz running original Game Boy software for backward compatibility.
The PlayStation 2's Emotion Engine CPU ran at 294 MHz and used a 128-bit SIMD co-processor; the Graphics Synthesizer rendered at up to 75 million polygons per second.
The PS2 used a proprietary 'MagicGate' DRM scheme on its memory cards and DVD discs to prevent unauthorized software, implemented via a Toshiba encryption chip.
The Nintendo GameCube's memory card slot used a proprietary Matsushita-designed card format; data corruption was common if the card was removed mid-save, unlike internal flash.
The Wii's motion-sensing MotionPlus accessory added a Epson Toyocom IDG-600 MEMs gyroscope to the Wii Remote to measure rotational speed, correcting drift from the accelerometer alone.
The Nintendo Switch's Joy-Con controllers each contain an accelerometer and gyroscope, and the right Joy-Con includes an NFC reader for amiibo support plus an IR motion camera.
The Switch's dock does not contain any processing hardware; it only provides power, HDMI output conversion, and USB ports, with all computation occurring in the tablet unit itself.
The Virtual Boy used a 32-bit NEC V810 CPU at 20 MHz and produced its 3D effect via red LED arrays scanned by oscillating mirrors rather than an actual display panel.
The Virtual Boy's monochrome red display was chosen because red LEDs were the cheapest and brightest available in 1995; Nintendo called the color scheme 'Virtual Reality'.
The Philips CD-i used a 15 MHz Motorola 68000 CPU and was designed as a multimedia CD-ROM player; its controller was criticized as one of the worst ever designed for video games.
The Vectrex had a built-in 9-inch vector monitor, the only home console to include its own display; it used a 1.5 MHz Motorola 6809 CPU and generated pure vector graphics.
The Intellivision used a General Instrument CP1610 16-bit CPU at 894 kHz paired with the AY-3-8900 STIC chip for graphics, giving it 16 moving objects per frame.
The ColecoVision used a Zilog Z80 CPU at 3.58 MHz and a Texas Instruments TMS9928 VDP capable of displaying 15 colors with up to 32 hardware sprites.
The Magnavox Odyssey, released in 1972, used no CPU or ROM chips — its circuits were entirely analog logic built from transistors, diodes, and resistors on a single board.
The Fairchild Channel F, released in 1976, was the first cartridge-based home console and used the Fairchild F8 CPU at 1.79 MHz, predating the Atari 2600 by a year.
The Bally Astrocade used a Zilog Z80 CPU and a custom UART-based graphics chip that generated graphics via a color lookup table, unusual for hardware of its 1977 era.
The Super Nintendo's multitap adapter allowed up to 5 players by connecting to one controller port, using a polling protocol that the SNES processed sequentially.
The Sega Activator was a full-body motion controller released for the Genesis in 1993, using eight infrared beams to detect limb movements; it was notoriously inaccurate.
The Nintendo Power Glove used ultrasonic sensors on the TV and finger-bend sensors in the glove to detect hand position; despite poor accuracy, it became a pop-culture icon.
The Sega 32X required its own separate power adapter, an AV cable, and a pass-through connector on the Genesis, resulting in a cable-heavy setup many consumers found unappealing.
The N64 Expansion Pak plugged into the memory expansion port and doubled RAM from 4 MB to 8 MB; it was required to run certain games and enabled higher-resolution modes in others.
The PlayStation's Dual Analog controller, released in 1997, added two analog sticks with a mode button to switch between digital and analog inputs — a precursor to the DualShock.
The DualShock controller added two rumble motors (a large eccentric mass and a small one) to the Dual Analog form factor, providing asymmetric vibration feedback.
The Xbox 360's wireless controllers used a proprietary 2.4 GHz protocol rather than Bluetooth, allowing up to 4 controllers per console without pairing conflicts.
The Dreamcast VMU (Visual Memory Unit) was a memory card with its own LCD screen, speaker, D-pad, and two action buttons — it functioned as a standalone handheld game device.
The N64 cartridges used mask ROM chips that were significantly more expensive to manufacture than PlayStation's CD-ROMs, contributing to higher game prices and smaller storage capacity.
The Nintendo 64DD add-on used rewritable magnetic disk cartridges holding 64 MB, but it launched only in Japan in 1999 and sold poorly due to limited software.
The GD-ROM format used by Dreamcast held approximately 1 GB of data — nearly 7× a standard CD — and used a different sector layout to complicate copying, though this was eventually defeated.
The Xbox 360's proprietary hard drives used standard 2.5-inch SATA drives internally but were enclosed in custom shells with a digital key in firmware preventing standard drives from working.
PS3 was the first console to ship with a Blu-ray drive as standard; the drive read at 2× speed (72 Mbit/s), which Sony used partly to justify the console's high price.
The GameBoy Advance cartridges used 32-pin edge connectors and contained flash ROM chips; some cartridges included a real-time clock chip with its own coin-cell battery.
The PS3 launch models consumed up to 380 watts of power — more than many desktop PCs of the time — due to the complex Cell processor and early 90nm manufacturing process.
The Xbox 360's power brick was external and weighed approximately 1.1 kg; it converted mains voltage to multiple DC rails because the console's internal power delivery was too complex.
The Nintendo Switch draws approximately 18 watts when docked and under load, far less than PS4 or Xbox One, partly due to its mobile-derived Tegra SoC.
The Game Boy Color used two AA batteries and drew so little current that the batteries often lasted over 10 hours, making them a reliable device for long travel.
The NES had two main hardware revisions: the toaster-style front-loader (NES-001) with a ZIF-like connector prone to pin corrosion, and the top-loader (NES-101) with a more reliable 72-pin connector.
The SNES 1 CHIP revision, introduced mid-production, replaced three separate PPU chips with a single chip; it produced a sharper video signal and is preferred by enthusiasts.
The PS2 Slim (SCPH-70000 series) reduced the console's volume by 75% and its weight from 2.2 kg to 0.9 kg, by integrating the power supply into the main board and using a slimmer optical drive.
The PlayStation 3 Super Slim reduced the chassis height by eliminating the sliding door for the optical drive, replacing it with a manual top-loading tray that critics considered less premium.
The Game Boy Pocket redesigned the original Game Boy in a thinner form factor using only 2 AAA batteries instead of 4 AA batteries, and upgraded the screen to a cleaner STN LCD.
Sega released the Genesis Model 3 (by Majesco) with a smaller form factor but no headphone jack, no cartridge EXT port, and no Sega CD compatibility, making it a cost-reduced revision.
The Wii Mini, released in Canada in 2012, removed GameCube backward compatibility, the SD card slot, and Wi-Fi connectivity, targeting the budget market as an entry-level console.
Arcade CRT monitors typically used tri-gun shadow mask or aperture grille designs at resolutions of 240p or 480i; the phosphor dot pitch affected perceived sharpness at different resolutions.
The Nintendo DS's lower touchscreen used a 4-wire resistive touch panel requiring physical pressure, which is why the stylus worked but capacitive (finger) touch had reduced accuracy.
The Game Boy Advance SP (AGS-001) launched with a front-lit screen, not a true backlight; the backlit AGS-101 revision came later and is considered significantly superior.
The 3DS's glasses-free 3D screen used a parallax barrier layer — a grid of vertical slits that directed slightly different images to each eye based on viewing angle.
The Virtual Boy used oscillating mirrors vibrating at approximately 50 Hz to scan a column of LEDs across the display area, creating a full frame from a single vertical strip of light.
The Super Nintendo's SPC700 sound chip, designed by Sony, contained its own 64 KB of dedicated audio RAM and ran a separate instruction set from the main CPU entirely.
The Sega Genesis's YM2612 FM synthesis chip produced six FM channels of audio using four operators per channel, delivering a metallic, buzzy timbre characteristic of the system.
The Neo Geo's Yamaha YM2610 sound chip combined FM synthesis with ADPCM sample playback and SSG (square wave) channels, giving it a richer audio palette than most competitors.
The PlayStation used a custom SPU (Sound Processing Unit) with 24 hardware channels of ADPCM audio, hardware reverb effects, and its own 512 KB of dedicated sound RAM.
The GameCube's Macronix DSP chip delivered 64 simultaneous audio channels and hardware Dolby Pro Logic II encoding, enabling surround sound output over an analog connection.
The Dreamcast's AICA audio chip combined an ARM7 audio CPU with 64 channels of ADPCM, 128 MB of sound RAM, and built-in hardware MIDI synthesis capability.
The Nintendo 64 used Rambus RDRAM (RDRAM) running at 500 MHz on a 9-bit bus; the high bandwidth was key to feeding the RCP, though it added cost and latency.
The PS2 had a split memory architecture: 32 MB of RDRAM as main RAM, 4 MB of eDRAM on the Graphics Synthesizer die, and 2 MB of fast DRAM on the IOP subsystem.
The Xbox's memory subsystem used 64 MB of DDR SDRAM on a shared 128-bit bus used by both CPU and GPU, allowing more flexible memory allocation than fixed split designs.
The Wii's Hollywood GPU included 3 MB of embedded 1T-SRAM for texture cache, backed by 64 MB of external GDDR3 as main RAM shared between CPU and GPU.
The Saturn's VDP1 sprite chip had 512 KB of framebuffer VRAM and 512 KB of texture VRAM, while the VDP2 background chip had 512 KB of VRAM — all separate memory pools.
The original NES used an RF modulator for video output; it also had an expansion port on the bottom that was only used in Japan for the Family Computer Disk System.
The Sega Genesis had a 9-pin DE-9 controller port, deliberately compatible with Atari joystick pinouts, and also featured a 9-pin serial EXT port for accessories like the Mega Modem.
The PS2 included two USB 1.1 ports and an i.Link (FireWire IEEE 1394) port on the front panel, though i.Link was only supported by a handful of games.
The GameCube's high-speed Serial Port 1 was capable of delivering 128 Mbit/s and was used by the broadband adapter and modem adapter accessories for online connectivity.
The Dreamcast's modem used a standard 56K V.90 chip but was housed in an expansion bay so it could be replaced with a broadband adapter using 10Base-T Ethernet.
The Nintendo DS's wireless networking supported only WEP security due to the limited cryptographic capabilities of its 802.11 implementation chip, making it incompatible with modern WPA2 routers.
The PSP included an 802.11b wireless card and a USB 2.0 port for data transfer, plus an infrared port and a proprietary AV cable connector for TV output.
The Xbox 360's HDMI port was only added in 2007 with the Elite model; original Core and Premium units shipped with only composite, S-Video, and component outputs.

The NES's 72-pin cartridge connector was known for developing poor contacts over time; the common fix was to bend the pins back into position, though Nintendo's official advice was to blow into cartridges.
The SNES cartridge region lockout used a physical key notch — the cartridge shape differed between regions — plus software region checks, requiring both to be bypassed for import play.
The PlayStation's controller port used a serial bit-bang protocol at around 250 kHz; the same protocol was used for the memory card slots, using only clock, data, and chip-select lines.
Saturn games were vulnerable to swap disc attacks because the optical drive firmware did not recheck region or authentication after the initial boot, allowing unencrypted imports to run.
The Atari 2600's TIA chip required the CPU to spend roughly 22 cycles per scanline just managing the display, leaving only 76 cycles per line for game logic — a severe constraint.
The Famicom Disk System used floppy disks with a custom RF5C296-derived drive controller and added RAM expansion, helping offset the higher cost of ROM cartridges in Japan in 1986.
The PlayStation 1 used a double-speed (2×) CD-ROM drive with a custom data format for error correction; early SCPH-1000 units had a notoriously fragile laser mechanism.
The Sega Saturn's main board went through multiple revisions; early VA0 revisions ran hot and were less reliable, while later VA-SG revisions consolidated chips and reduced power draw.
The Dreamcast's GD-ROM drive mechanism was manufactured by Yamaha and was known for lens degradation over time, causing disc read errors that increased with unit age.
Dedicated arcade cabinets used either upright, cocktail (table), or sit-down (cockpit) form factors; cocktail cabinets flipped the screen 180 degrees between players on a shared display.
Arcade trackballs in cabinets like Centipede used optical encoders — slotted disks interrupting infrared beams as the ball rolled — to convert mechanical rotation to digital pulse counts.
Coin mechanisms in arcade machines used optical or magnetic sensors to distinguish real coins from slugs; the reject mechanism returned invalid coins through a separate tube.
Arcade control panels were typically mounted on 0.75-inch plywood or steel plates; the mounting hardware for joysticks used square cutouts to prevent rotation under heavy use.
Steering wheel arcade cabinets like those in Sega's racing games used a magnetic induction mechanism to provide force feedback, simulating road resistance through the wheel.
Light gun arcade games used a photodiode in the gun barrel that detected the brightness pulse from a white frame drawn on the CRT, calculating hit position from timing.
Arcade monitor deflection boards amplified the small control signals from the game PCB into the high-voltage sweep signals needed to drive the CRT's horizontal and vertical deflection coils.
JAMMA-standard power supplies in arcade cabinets supplied +5V for logic, +12V for monitors and motors, and -5V for certain legacy sound circuits, with each rail rated for several amps.
The PS Vita's OLED screen (original PCH-1000 model) offered superior contrast and color compared to the LCD used in the slim PCH-2000 revision, making the original model sought after.
The PS Vita included both a front touchscreen and a rear touch pad on the back of the unit, enabling games to use pinching or multi-touch gestures spanning both surfaces.
The PS Vita had built-in GPS in the 3G model (PCH-1100), using the SIM card slot for location data in supported applications — an unusual feature for a dedicated gaming handheld.
The original Xbox One included a mandatory Kinect 2.0 sensor, requiring an always-connected 3-meter USB 3.0 cable; Microsoft later removed the Kinect requirement after consumer backlash.
The Xbox One's SoC combined a custom AMD Jaguar 8-core CPU and a GCN-based GPU (1.31 teraflops) on a single AMD die manufactured at 28nm by TSMC.
The Xbox One S introduced HDR support and 4K Blu-ray playback while eliminating the external power brick by integrating the PSU into the console, reducing overall size.
The PS4's DualShock 4 was the first PlayStation controller to include a touchpad (1920×900 resolution), a light bar for camera tracking, and a mono speaker.
The PS4 uses a semi-custom AMD APU with 8 Jaguar CPU cores and 18 GCN compute units delivering 1.84 teraflops, paired with 8 GB of GDDR5 RAM on a unified 256-bit bus.
The PS4's 'share' button allowed instant video capture and streaming to Twitch or Ustream by offloading encoding to a dedicated MPEG-4 encoder chip separate from the main SoC.
The 3DO Interactive Multiplayer used a RISC CPU and custom graphics hardware designed by The 3DO Company; multiple manufacturers (Panasonic, Goldstar, Sanyo) built units to the same spec.
The Bandai Playdia used a custom Hitachi SH-1 CPU and single-speed CD-ROM drive; it targeted young children and used a unique D-pad-less controller with only direction-labeled buttons.
The Casio Loopy, released in 1995 only in Japan, included a built-in thermal sticker printer and used a Hitachi SH-1 CPU; it was marketed exclusively to young girls.
The Sega Pico used a Genesis-derived CPU but featured a book-shaped flat surface with a touchpad for drawing and pointing, targeting preschool children with educational software.
The Action Max, released in 1987 by Worlds of Wonder, read audio tones recorded on VHS tapes to trigger scoring events, requiring it to be connected to a real VHS player.
The RDI Halcyon, released in 1985 for $2,500, used a LaserDisc player as its media system and featured speech recognition for game control — the first home console with voice commands.
The PS5's SSD uses a custom flash controller achieving 5.5 GB/s raw throughput and up to 9 GB/s with compression, eliminating traditional streaming installation and loading screens.
The Xbox Series X includes a 1 TB custom NVMe SSD using a Velocity Architecture with hardware decompression, achieving roughly 4.8 GB/s sustained read speeds.
The Xbox Series S uses the same Zen 2 CPU cores as the Series X but a smaller RDNA 2 GPU with only 4 teraflops, paired with 10 GB of GDDR6 and a 512 GB NVMe SSD.
The Switch OLED model (2021) upgraded the screen to a 7-inch OLED panel with richer colors but retained the same Tegra X1 processor as the original Switch.
The Nintendo Switch Lite, released in 2019, removed the detachable Joy-Con system and dock support, integrating the controls directly into the smaller handheld-only chassis.
